title: Install dependencies and configure LLVM/CompilerGym environment
description: Set up the Python environment with all required libraries. CompilerGym provides a convenient interface to LLVM's opt tool and benchmark programs. If CompilerGym is unavailable or broken, fall back to direct LLVM opt invocation with manually compiled benchmarks.

steps.step1: Create a Python virtual environment: python3 -m venv venv && source venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install core dependencies: pip install numpy scipy pandas matplotlib seaborn shapiq networkx scikit-learn
steps.step3: Attempt to install CompilerGym: pip install compiler_gym. Test with: python -c "import compiler_gym; env = compiler_gym.make('llvm-v0'); env.reset(); print('CompilerGym OK')"
steps.step4: If CompilerGym fails, install LLVM tools: sudo apt-get install llvm-14 clang-14 (or available version). Verify: opt --version
steps.step5: If using direct LLVM: download cBench source files from CompilerGym's dataset or a public mirror. Compile each to LLVM IR (.bc files) using clang -O0 -emit-llvm -c
steps.step6: Create a project directory structure: mkdir -p results/{data,figures,tables,logs} src/
steps.step7: Verify setup by running a simple test: apply a single pass to a benchmark and measure IR instruction count change

title: Select benchmark programs and candidate passes
description: Identify the benchmark programs and LLVM optimization passes to use. Select a diverse subset of cBench programs covering different domains. Screen passes by individual marginal contribution to identify the top-K most impactful ones.

steps.step1: Load the cBench benchmark suite via CompilerGym (or from compiled .bc files). Target 10-15 programs covering: signal processing (e.g., adpcm, gsm, jpeg), cryptography (e.g., blowfish, sha, rijndael), text processing (e.g., bzip2, ispell), numerical (e.g., susan, qsort, bitcount, stringsearch). Record baseline IR instruction counts (unoptimized).
steps.step2: Enumerate candidate LLVM optimization passes. Start with the ~60 passes included in LLVM -O3. Key passes to consider: mem2reg, instcombine, simplifycfg, gvn, licm, loop-unroll, loop-rotate, loop-simplify, indvars, sroa, sccp, dce, adce, reassociate, inline, jump-threading, correlated-propagation, early-cse, loop-vectorize, slp-vectorizer, tailcallelim, mergereturn, globalopt, constmerge, deadargelim, argpromotion, ipsccp.
steps.step3: Screen each pass individually: for each benchmark, apply each pass alone and record IR instruction count reduction. Rank passes by average marginal contribution across benchmarks.
steps.step4: Select top K=20 passes based on screening. If some passes have near-zero individual effect but are known to be enabling (e.g., mem2reg, loop-simplify), include them anyway as they may show strong interaction effects.
steps.step5: Save the selected pass list and per-benchmark screening results to results/data/pass_screening.json. Record the unoptimized baseline instruction counts for all benchmarks.
steps.step6: Compute and record the -O1, -O2, -O3, -Os, -Oz instruction counts for all benchmarks as reference baselines.

title: Define the value function and sampling infrastructure
description: Implement the cooperative game value function v(S) that maps a subset of passes to a performance metric. Build the sampling infrastructure needed by shapiq for Shapley interaction computation.

steps.step1: Implement value_function(benchmark, pass_subset) -> float that: (a) takes a benchmark identifier and a subset of passes, (b) applies the passes in a fixed canonical order (matching LLVM -O3 pipeline order) to the benchmark's unoptimized IR, (c) returns the IR instruction count reduction as a fraction: (baseline_count - optimized_count) / baseline_count. Use a fixed pass ordering to isolate the effect of pass selection from pass ordering.
steps.step2: Implement a wrapper class compatible with shapiq's game interface. The shapiq library expects a callable that takes a binary vector x in {0,1}^K (indicating which passes are on/off) and returns the value. Implement this as: game(x) = value_function(benchmark, passes_where_x_is_1).
steps.step3: Test the value function on 5 random subsets for 3 benchmarks. Verify that: (a) empty set gives 0 reduction, (b) full set gives reduction close to -O3, (c) values are deterministic (same subset gives same result).
steps.step4: Measure the average time per value function evaluation. Target: <0.5 seconds per evaluation. If slower, consider caching or reducing benchmark size.
steps.step5: Save the game wrapper code to src/game.py and test results to results/data/value_function_validation.json.

title: Compute Shapley Interaction Indices (orders 1-3)
description: Use the shapiq library to compute Shapley values (order 1), pairwise Shapley interaction indices (order 2), and third-order interaction indices (order 3) for each benchmark program. This is the core computation of the paper. Use 3 random seeds for the sampling-based approximation to assess convergence.

steps.step1: For each of the 10-15 benchmark programs and each of 3 random seeds (42, 123, 456): instantiate a shapiq approximator (KernelSHAP-IQ or PermutationSamplingSII) with max_order=3 and budget=3000 game evaluations per benchmark.
steps.step2: Compute the Shapley interaction indices. shapiq returns InteractionValues objects containing phi values at each order. Extract: (a) order-1 Shapley values phi_i for each pass i, (b) order-2 indices phi_{i,j} for each pass pair, (c) order-3 indices phi_{i,j,k} for each pass triple.
steps.step3: Store all interaction values per benchmark per seed in results/data/interactions/. Save as JSON with structure: {benchmark, seed, order_1: {pass: value}, order_2: {(pass_i, pass_j): value}, order_3: {(pass_i, pass_j, pass_k): value}}.
steps.step4: Compute convergence diagnostics: for each benchmark, compare interaction values across the 3 seeds. Report mean and standard deviation. Flag any interactions where std/|mean| > 0.5 as poorly converged.
steps.step5: Estimated runtime: 10-15 benchmarks × 3 seeds × 3000 evaluations × ~0.3s/eval = ~27,000-40,500 seconds = 7.5-11.25 hours on 1 core. With 2 cores running benchmarks in parallel: ~4-6 hours. If too slow, reduce budget to 2000 evaluations or reduce to 8 benchmarks.
steps.step6: Implement parallel execution: use Python multiprocessing (2 workers) to process benchmarks in parallel. Each worker handles a different benchmark.

title: Variance decomposition analysis
description: Decompose the performance variance into contributions from individual effects (order 1), pairwise interactions (order 2), and higher-order interactions (order 3+). This directly tests the core hypothesis.

steps.step1: For each benchmark, compute the sum of squared Shapley values at each order: SS_k = sum of phi_S^2 for all interactions S of size k. This gives the variance explained by each interaction order.
steps.step2: Compute the fraction of total interaction variance at each order: frac_k = SS_k / (SS_1 + SS_2 + SS_3). Report for each benchmark and averaged across benchmarks.
steps.step3: Additionally, compute the R-squared of predicting v(S) from a linear model using only order-1 terms, then order-1+2, then order-1+2+3. This gives a complementary variance decomposition. Sample 500 random subsets per benchmark and fit the models.
steps.step4: Test the hypothesis: is the additional variance explained by order-3 interactions >= 10%? Compute a one-sample t-test across benchmarks with H0: additional_variance_order3 <= 5% (the refutation threshold).

title: Interaction structure analysis
description: Analyze the discovered interaction patterns: identify top synergistic and redundant pass groups, assess cross-program stability of interactions.

steps.step1: For each benchmark, rank all order-2 interactions by magnitude. Identify the top-10 most synergistic (largest positive phi_{i,j}) and top-10 most redundant (largest negative phi_{i,j}) pass pairs.
steps.step2: For each benchmark, rank all order-3 interactions by magnitude. Identify the top-10 most synergistic and top-10 most redundant pass triples.
steps.step3: Cross-program stability: for each pass pair (i,j), compute the correlation of phi_{i,j} across benchmarks. Identify 'universal' interactions (consistent sign and rank across >80% of benchmarks) vs. 'program-specific' interactions (sign changes across programs).
steps.step4: Repeat cross-program stability analysis for order-3 interactions. Focus on triples that appear in the top-10 for multiple benchmarks.
steps.step5: Categorize the discovered interactions by pass type: (a) loop optimizations (loop-unroll, loop-rotate, licm, indvars, loop-simplify), (b) scalar optimizations (instcombine, gvn, sccp, early-cse, reassociate), (c) cleanup passes (dce, adce, simplifycfg), (d) interprocedural (inline, globalopt, ipsccp, deadargelim). Analyze whether interactions cluster within or across categories.

title: Interaction-guided pass selection algorithm
description: Implement the proposed interaction-guided greedy pass selection algorithm and its variants. This algorithm uses the computed Shapley interaction indices to select a near-optimal subset of passes.

steps.step1: Implement greedy_individual_selection(phi_order1, budget_k): greedily select top-k passes by individual Shapley value. This is the simplest baseline.
steps.step2: Implement greedy_pairwise_selection(phi_order1, phi_order2, budget_k): start with the highest-value pass, then at each step add the pass maximizing: phi_i + sum_{j in selected} phi_{i,j}. This uses pairwise interactions.
steps.step3: Implement greedy_interaction_selection(phi_order1, phi_order2, phi_order3, budget_k): same as pairwise but also add the three-way interaction terms: phi_i + sum_{j in S} phi_{i,j} + sum_{j,k in S} phi_{i,j,k}. This is the proposed method.
steps.step4: Implement synergy_seeded_selection(phi_order1, phi_order2, phi_order3, budget_k): identify the top-3 highest-synergy triples, seed the selection with the best triple, then greedily add remaining passes using the full interaction scoring.
steps.step5: Implement redundancy_pruning(selected_passes, phi_order2, phi_order3, threshold): post-processing step that removes passes from the selected set if their total negative interaction with the rest exceeds a threshold. Apply after each greedy method.
steps.step6: For each benchmark: run all selection methods with budget k = 5, 8, 10, 12, 15 passes. Evaluate selected subsets using the value function. Record IR instruction count reduction for each method and budget.
steps.step7: Save all algorithms to src/selection.py and results to results/data/selection_results.json.

title: Baseline comparisons
description: Run baseline methods for fair comparison: LLVM default optimization levels, random search, and genetic algorithm with equivalent computational budget.

steps.step1: Record LLVM default optimization levels: -O1, -O2, -O3, -Os, -Oz instruction counts for all benchmarks (already computed in Data Preparation step 6).
steps.step2: Implement random search baseline: for each benchmark, sample 1000 random pass subsets of sizes 5, 8, 10, 12, 15. Record the best IR reduction found. Use 3 random seeds (42, 123, 456).
steps.step3: Implement genetic algorithm baseline: population=50, generations=20, crossover_rate=0.7, mutation_rate=0.1, tournament_size=3. Binary encoding (each gene = pass on/off). Run for budgets of 1000 evaluations per benchmark. Use 3 random seeds.
steps.step4: Ensure fair comparison: all methods are evaluated using the same value function with the same fixed pass ordering. Record wall-clock time for each method.
steps.step6: Estimated runtime: random search = 10 benchmarks × 3 seeds × 5 budgets × 1000 evals × 0.3s = 45,000s on 1 core. With 2 cores: ~6.25 hours. OPTIMIZATION: reuse the 3000 evaluations from Shapley computation as the random sample (they are random subsets). This reduces additional baseline computation to just the GA: 10 × 3 × 1000 × 0.3s = 9000s ≈ 2.5 hours on 1 core, 1.25 hours on 2 cores.

title: Ablation study: interaction order
description: Systematically ablate the interaction order used in the selection algorithm to quantify the value of each order of interaction. This directly tests whether higher-order interactions improve pass selection.

steps.step1: For each benchmark and each budget k in {5, 8, 10, 12, 15}: run greedy selection using (a) order-1 only, (b) order-1+2, (c) order-1+2+3. Record IR reduction for each.
steps.step2: Compute paired differences: (order-1+2+3 vs order-1+2) and (order-1+2 vs order-1) for each benchmark. Report mean improvement and standard error across benchmarks.
steps.step3: Statistical test: paired t-test for each pair of methods across benchmarks. Report p-values and effect sizes (Cohen's d).

title: Ablation study: number of candidate passes (K)
description: Vary the number of candidate passes K to understand its impact on interaction quality and selection effectiveness.

steps.step1: Run the full pipeline (Shapley computation + interaction-guided selection) for K = 10, 15, 20 passes on 5 representative benchmarks.
steps.step2: For each K, measure: (a) time for Shapley computation, (b) variance decomposition fractions, (c) selection performance at budget k=10.
steps.step3: Analyze the tradeoff: more passes means richer interaction structure but noisier estimates (fixed evaluation budget spread over more interactions).

title: Ablation study: evaluation budget for Shapley estimation
description: Vary the number of game evaluations used for Shapley interaction approximation to understand convergence behavior.

steps.step1: For 5 representative benchmarks: compute Shapley interactions with budgets of 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 5000 evaluations (3 seeds each).
steps.step2: For each budget, measure: (a) mean absolute difference in interaction values compared to the highest budget (5000), (b) selection performance using the estimated interactions, (c) wall-clock time.
steps.step3: Identify the minimum budget that achieves within 95% of the best selection performance. This informs practical deployment.

title: Transferability analysis
description: Test whether interaction patterns computed on one program can improve pass selection for similar programs, enabling amortization of the Shapley computation cost.

steps.step1: Compute pairwise cosine similarity between benchmarks using their Shapley interaction vectors (concatenation of order-1, order-2, order-3 values, flattened). Cluster benchmarks using hierarchical clustering.
steps.step2: Leave-one-out transfer: for each benchmark B, use the interaction indices averaged over all OTHER benchmarks in the same cluster to perform interaction-guided selection for B. Compare to using B's own interactions (oracle) and to using interactions from a random other benchmark (control).
steps.step3: Report transfer success rate: fraction of benchmarks where transferred interactions achieve >= 90% of oracle selection performance.

title: Statistical evaluation of results against success criteria
description: Compile all experimental results and evaluate against the pre-defined success criteria from the hypothesis.

steps.step1: Criterion 1 — Significant higher-order interactions: count the fraction of order-3 interactions with |phi_{i,j,k}| > 2 * std_across_seeds. Report whether >= 30% of pass triples have significant third-order interactions.
steps.step2: Criterion 2 — Variance explained: report the average additional variance explained by order-3 interactions beyond pairwise. Evaluate whether >= 10%.
steps.step3: Criterion 3 — Selection performance: for each benchmark at budget k=10, count whether interaction-guided (order-1+2+3) outperforms pairwise-only (order-1+2). Report the win rate across benchmarks. Evaluate whether >= 60%.
steps.step4: Compute overall comparison table: for each method (LLVM-O1, -O2, -O3, -Os, -Oz, random search, GA, individual greedy, pairwise greedy, full interaction greedy, synergy-seeded) and each benchmark, report IR reduction. Compute mean ± std across benchmarks.
steps.step5: Run Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for pairwise method comparisons (non-parametric, appropriate for small N). Report p-values with Bonferroni correction.
steps.step6: Save comprehensive results to results/tables/main_results.csv and results/data/statistical_tests.json.

title: Generate figures and tables for the paper
description: Create publication-quality figures and tables summarizing all experimental results.

steps.step1: Figure 1 — Variance decomposition bar chart: stacked bar chart showing fraction of variance at order 1, 2, 3 for each benchmark. Use consistent color scheme (blue=order1, orange=order2, red=order3).
steps.step2: Figure 2 — Interaction heatmap: for 2-3 representative benchmarks, plot a heatmap of pairwise Shapley interaction indices (passes × passes). Annotate the top-5 strongest interactions.
steps.step3: Figure 3 — Top higher-order interactions: horizontal bar chart showing the top-10 strongest order-3 interactions across all benchmarks, with error bars across seeds.
steps.step4: Figure 4 — Selection performance comparison: grouped bar chart comparing all methods (LLVM levels, baselines, ablated versions, proposed method) across benchmarks. Use consistent colors. Also create a line plot showing performance vs. pass budget k.
steps.step5: Figure 5 — Interaction network: for 1-2 representative benchmarks, visualize passes as nodes and interactions as edges (width proportional to |phi|, color for sign). Use networkx spring layout.
steps.step6: Figure 6 — Transferability: heatmap of cosine similarity between benchmarks' interaction vectors. Overlay cluster boundaries.
steps.step7: Figure 7 — Convergence: line plot of selection performance vs. evaluation budget (from ablation study).
steps.step8: Table 1 — Main comparison table: rows = benchmarks, columns = methods, cells = IR reduction %. Bold the best, underline second-best. Include mean ± std row.
steps.step9: Table 2 — Top synergistic and redundant pass groups: list top-5 order-2 and top-5 order-3 interactions with their values and interpretations.
steps.step10: Save all figures to results/figures/ as PDF and PNG (300 DPI). Use matplotlib with seaborn styling. Font size >= 10pt for readability.
steps.step11: Use consistent styling across all figures: matplotlib rcParams with font.size=12, axes.labelsize=14, figure.dpi=300, savefig.bbox='tight'.
